network.overall_strategy: This network treats glutamate as a modular, convergent product of coupled hydrothermal, surface, and prebiotic environments rather than as a single one-pot synthesis. The dominant logic follows the literature: hydrothermal Fe0/FeS/greigite chemistry reduces CO2 to formate and pyruvate; pyruvate and glyoxylate converge through protometabolic chemistry to alpha-ketoglutarate; alpha-ketoglutarate is then converted to glutamate by multiple experimentally demonstrated routes including transamination, FeS/H2S reductive amination, H2/Ni reductive amination, NADH-mediated reductive amination, and pyrite-photocatalyzed amination. In parallel, lower-selectivity surface routes contribute background glutamate through acrolein-based Strecker chemistry, HCN oligomerization/hydrolysis, spark-discharge/UV atmospheric synthesis, and cyanosulfidic nitrile chemistry. The network explicitly encodes the literature caveat that integrated one-pot glutamate production is kinetically disfavored, so productive pathways are separated into temporally or spatially distinct modules.

reactions.name: Fe0 and greigite mediated reduction of CO2 to formate

conditions.pressure: aqueous, hydrothermal; near ambient to elevated
conditions.other: anoxic alkaline hydrothermal conditions; serpentinization-derived H2

reactions.mechanism: Surface-mediated electron transfer reduces CO2 to C1 carboxylate species on iron-sulfur and native iron phases.
reactions.reasoning: Varma et al. (2018) showed native iron reduces CO2 to formate, acetate, and pyruvate at 30-100C in water; greigite and related Fe-S minerals catalyze CO2 reduction under hydrothermal conditions (Roldan et al., 2015; Preiner et al., 2020). This is a literature-grounded entry point to reduced carbon.

reactions.name: Hydrothermal formation of pyruvate from CO2-derived reduced carbon

conditions.pressure: aqueous, hydrothermal; near ambient to elevated
conditions.other: anoxic conditions; protometabolic CO2 fixation products observed in iron-driven systems

reactions.mechanism: Iron-promoted CO2 fixation yields C3 keto acids within acetyl-CoA-like and protometabolic reaction manifolds.
reactions.reasoning: Varma et al. (2018) reported pyruvate among products from CO2 reduced by native iron in aqueous prebiotic conditions. Magnetite is included as a hydrothermal mineral catalyst consistent with Fe-bearing vent mineralogy and Fischer-Tropsch-like carbon fixation contexts discussed in the broader hydrothermal literature.

reactions.name: Surface photochemical generation of HCN from reduced atmospheric mixtures

conditions.other: UV-irradiated reduced atmospheric or volcanic gas feedstock context; reaction node represents atmospheric-to-pool delivery rather than stoichiometrically complete gas chemistry

reactions.mechanism: UV-driven photochemistry in reduced C/N gas mixtures generates HCN that accumulates in surface reservoirs and is further concentrated by evaporation or freezing.
reactions.reasoning: HCN is required by the literature-supported Strecker, HCN oligomerization, and cyanosulfidic routes. Its atmospheric production is supported by Miller (1955), Sagan and Khare (1971), Johnson et al. (2008), and Parker et al. (2011). This node is intentionally abstracted as environmental delivery because the cited studies use gas mixtures beyond the explicitly tracked network molecules.

reactions.name: Surface photochemical formation of acrolein in spark and UV processed reduced mixtures

conditions.other: UV/spark-derived atmospheric organics delivered to surface waters; reduced carbon feed gases implicit from Miller-Urey-type experiments

reactions.mechanism: Energetic atmospheric processing yields reactive aldehydes including acrolein, which then enter aqueous surface chemistry.
reactions.reasoning: Van Trump and Miller (1972) identified acrolein as the key precursor in glutamate Strecker chemistry, and Parker et al. (2011) confirmed this mechanism by reanalysis of Miller samples. The exact gas-phase formation sequence is complex, so this reaction encodes environmental production and capture of acrolein rather than an oversimplified stoichiometric gas reaction.

reactions.name: HCN oligomerization and hydrolytic release of glyoxylate-containing product pool

conditions.other: HCN concentration above ~0.01 M, favored by eutectic freezing or evaporative concentration; alkaline pH around 9

reactions.mechanism: Concentrated HCN oligomerizes to complex nitrile polymers; upon hydrolysis, mixed carbonyl/carboxylate products including glyoxylate-like C2 oxoacids are released.
reactions.reasoning: Ferris et al. (1978) demonstrated that concentrated HCN oligomerization followed by hydrolysis yields diverse prebiotic organics including amino acids, while Sanchez et al. (1966) showed eutectic freezing can achieve required HCN concentrations. This node is conservatively framed as a mixed-product source feeding glyoxylate chemistry, avoiding the overclaim of direct selective glutamate formation.

reactions.name: Fe2+-promoted protometabolic coupling of pyruvate and glyoxylate to alpha-ketoglutarate

conditions.other: anoxic aqueous solution; TCA-like network within 3 h

reactions.mechanism: Protometabolic aldol/addition, dehydration, and redox transformations within an iron-promoted TCA-like network generate alpha-ketoglutarate from pyruvate and glyoxylate.
reactions.reasoning: Muchowska et al. (2019) showed Fe2+ promotes synthesis of 9 of 11 TCA intermediates, including alpha-ketoglutarate, from pyruvate and glyoxylate at 70C. This is the strongest literature-supported hub-generating step for glutamate-centered networks.

reactions.name: Metal-free protometabolic formation of alpha-ketoglutarate from pyruvate and glyoxylate
reactions.mechanism: Pyruvate and glyoxylate undergo metal-free aldol chemistry and internal redox chemistry to generate alpha-ketoglutarate analogs and related TCA intermediates.
reactions.reasoning: Stubbs et al. (2020) established a metal-free network where glyoxylate and pyruvate form TCA-like intermediates including alpha-ketoglutarate under pH 4.5 at 70C. This provides an independent convergent route to the same hub.

reactions.name: Acrolein-based Strecker progression to glutamate aminonitrile precursor pool

conditions.other: Michael addition of HCN to acrolein followed by aminonitrile-forming Strecker chemistry; dilute operation possible but concentration improves yield

reactions.mechanism: Acrolein undergoes conjugate addition with HCN to generate a nitrile-aldehyde precursor, followed by ammonia/cyanide addition to form a glutamate-related aminonitrile pool.
reactions.reasoning: Van Trump and Miller (1972) and Parker et al. (2011) identify acrolein as the key precursor to glutamate in classical prebiotic Strecker chemistry. This node explicitly represents the aminonitrile stage rather than collapsing directly to glutamate.

reactions.name: Hydrolysis of glutamate aminonitrile to glutamate

conditions.other: extended aqueous hydrolysis in anoxic pools

reactions.mechanism: Sequential nitrile hydrolysis converts aminonitrile functionality into carboxylate, yielding glutamate.
reactions.reasoning: The Strecker route to amino acids proceeds through aminonitrile hydrolysis; this is the mechanism inferred for glutamate from the acrolein pathway in Van Trump and Miller (1972) and confirmed in Parker et al. (2011).

reactions.name: Transamination of alpha-ketoglutarate with glycine to glutamate
reactions.mechanism: Amino transfer from glycine to alpha-ketoglutarate yields glutamate while converting the amino donor into glyoxylate.
reactions.reasoning: Stubbs et al. (2020) reported 44% yield of glutamate by transamination of alpha-ketoglutarate with glycine using AlK(SO4)2 at pH 5, 80C, 4 h. The production of glyoxylate as the paired keto acid makes this reaction a key convergence-and-recycling hub.

reactions.name: NADH-mediated reductive amination of alpha-ketoglutarate to glutamate
reactions.mechanism: Imine formation between alpha-ketoglutarate and ammonia is followed by hydride transfer from NADH to furnish glutamate.
reactions.reasoning: Nogal et al. (2024) obtained 33% yield for glutamate from alpha-ketoglutarate via NADH-mediated reductive amination at room temperature and pH above 7. This route is literature-demonstrated but requires a separately available reduced nicotinamide-like donor.

reactions.name: H2-driven nickel-catalyzed reductive amination of alpha-ketoglutarate

conditions.other: pH 8; individual step works, integrated one-pot network does not accumulate glutamate

reactions.mechanism: Nickel-mediated hydrogen activation enables reductive amination of alpha-ketoglutarate with ammonia under mild aqueous conditions.
reactions.reasoning: Kaur et al. (2024) demonstrated H2-driven reductive amination of alpha-ketoglutarate to glutamate at 22C, pH 8, 5 bar H2 over nickel catalysts and ground Aletai meteorite. The same study found glutamate not detectable in the one-pot integrated system, so this reaction is represented as a compartmentalized individual step.

reactions.name: FeS/H2S reductive amination of alpha-ketoglutarate to glutamate
reactions.mechanism: Iron sulfide surfaces facilitate imine formation and reduction of alpha-keto acids with ammonia to amino acids.
reactions.reasoning: Hafenbradl et al. (1995) showed FeS/H2S at 100C drives reductive amination of alpha-keto acids to amino acids; extension to alpha-ketoglutarate is directly in line with the reported scope and with later work on nonenzymatic alpha-keto acid amination.

reactions.name: Pyrite photocatalyzed amination of alpha-ketoglutarate to glutamate

conditions.other: anoxic UV-irradiated mineral surface; surface photoredox amination

reactions.mechanism: Photoexcited mineral surfaces promote reductive amination of alpha-keto acids with ammonia to amino acids.
reactions.reasoning: The deep research report cites a 2024 Nature Communications study showing UV-irradiated natural pyrite drives reductive amination of alpha-keto acids, yielding amino acids including glutamate and even enantiomeric excesses. This provides a distinct surface route from the alpha-ketoglutarate hub.

reactions.name: Fe0 and hydroxylamine promoted emergence of glutamate within the protometabolic pyruvate-glyoxylate network

conditions.other: anoxic aqueous network; glutamate detected among products rather than as a selective single-step product

reactions.mechanism: Within a complex iron-driven protometabolic network, pyruvate and glyoxylate generate TCA-like intermediates that, upon nitrogen incorporation from hydroxylamine, yield glutamate among several amino acids.
reactions.reasoning: Muchowska et al. (2019) reported that adding hydroxylamine and Fe0 to the pyruvate-glyoxylate network produced glutamate among amino-acid products. This is intentionally represented as a network-emergent route rather than an unrealistically selective elementary reaction.

reactions.name: HCN oligomerization and hydrolysis as a low-selectivity direct source of glutamate

conditions.other: 0.1 M HCN, pH 9.2; hydrolysis of oligomerized nitrile material; low selectivity

reactions.mechanism: Concentrated HCN oligomerizes into heterogeneous nitrile polymers; subsequent hydrolysis releases a broad amino acid mixture containing glutamate as a minor component.
reactions.reasoning: Ferris et al. (1978) showed that 0.1 M HCN at pH 9.2 forms oligomers whose hydrolysis releases glutamic acid among many products; Moser and Matthews (1968) similarly detected glutamic acid from hydrolysis of aminoacetonitrile polymers. This route is included as a minor background pathway only.

reactions.name: Spark-discharge and volcanic atmospheric synthesis delivering glutamate to surface reservoirs

conditions.temperature: variable atmospheric discharge; collection into 300-370K aqueous pools
conditions.other: reduced gas mixtures as in Miller-Urey-type systems and volcanic/H2S variants; product detected at low abundance

reactions.mechanism: Energetic atmospheric chemistry generates amino-acid mixtures that are trapped and concentrated in surface waters.
reactions.reasoning: Miller (1955) first identified glutamate chromatographically in spark-discharge products; Ring et al. (1972), Johnson et al. (2008), and Parker et al. (2011) confirmed glutamate in Miller-type experiments, and Sagan and Khare (1971) produced glutamate under UV photolysis of reduced gas mixtures. This is a direct but low-yield environmental source.

reactions.name: Cyanosulfidic photochemistry to acrylonitrile precursor pool

conditions.other: HCN plus reduced sulfur photoredox context; cyanosulfidic network conditions

reactions.mechanism: HCN-based photoredox sulfur chemistry yields activated nitriles including acrylonitrile within a broader precursor network.
reactions.reasoning: Patel et al. (2015) established cyanosulfidic chemistry from HCN and H2S under photoredox conditions, and Kuroda and Kobayashi (2024) discuss acrylonitrile-derived glutamate/glutamine precursor chemistry in this context. This is included as a literature-linked precursor route rather than a direct glutamate step.

reactions.name: Acrylonitrile progression to glutamate aminonitrile precursor pool

conditions.other: cyanosulfidic nitrile chemistry; precursor-forming rather than fully selective synthesis

reactions.mechanism: Activated nitriles undergo conjugate addition and aminonitrile-forming chemistry to yield C5 glutamate-related nitrile precursor pools.
reactions.reasoning: Kuroda and Kobayashi (2024) link acrylonitrile chemistry to glutamine/glutamate precursors in cyanosulfidic networks, grounded in the broader precursor framework of Patel et al. (2015). Because the exact sequence is not fully resolved, this route is explicitly treated as a precursor-pool step.

pathways.name: Fe2+-protometabolic alpha-ketoglutarate then transamination
pathways.description: Hydrothermal reduced carbon feeds a protometabolic Fe2+ network to alpha-ketoglutarate, followed by the highest-yield demonstrated glutamate-forming step.

pathways.name: Metal-free pyruvate-glyoxylate network then transamination
pathways.description: A metal-free warm aqueous network produces alpha-ketoglutarate from pyruvate and glyoxylate, then glycine transaminates it to glutamate.

pathways.name: Fe2+-protometabolism followed by NADH reductive amination
pathways.description: Alpha-ketoglutarate generated in the Fe2+ TCA-like network is converted to glutamate by NADH-mediated reductive amination at room temperature.

pathways.name: Fe2+-protometabolism followed by H2/Ni reductive amination
pathways.description: Hydrothermal and protometabolic modules converge on alpha-ketoglutarate, which is then aminated under mild H2/Ni conditions in a separated step.

pathways.name: Fe2+-protometabolism followed by FeS/H2S reductive amination
pathways.description: Alpha-ketoglutarate produced through protometabolic chemistry undergoes hydrothermal FeS/H2S reductive amination to glutamate.

pathways.name: Fe2+-protometabolism followed by pyrite-photocatalyzed amination
pathways.description: Alpha-ketoglutarate from the TCA-like network is converted on UV-irradiated pyrite surfaces to glutamate.

pathways.name: Acrolein Strecker pathway
pathways.description: Atmospheric/surface acrolein production feeds classical glutamate Strecker chemistry through a glutamate aminonitrile intermediate.

pathways.name: HCN oligomerization and hydrolysis background route
pathways.description: Concentrated HCN oligomerizes and hydrolyzes to a mixed organic inventory containing glutamate as a minor product.

pathways.name: Spark-discharge and UV atmospheric direct source
pathways.description: Miller-Urey-type atmospheric energy input generates glutamate directly at low abundance, followed by delivery to surface waters.

pathways.name: Cyanosulfidic acrylonitrile-to-aminonitrile route
pathways.description: HCN-based cyanosulfidic chemistry generates acrylonitrile, which progresses through nitrile precursor chemistry to glutamate aminonitrile and then glutamate.
